quiz_questions.question_text: What is a reference frame?

options.text: A fixed point in space
A type of laboratory equipment
A unit of measurement
A coordinate system used to measure position and motion

quiz_questions.explanation: A reference frame is a coordinate system with respect to which position, velocity, and other quantities are measured. All motion is relative to a chosen reference frame.

quiz_questions.question_text: How do you find the velocity of A relative to B?

options.text: v_AB = v_A - v_B
v_AB = v_A + v_B
v_AB = v_A / v_B
v_AB = v_A * v_B

quiz_questions.explanation: The velocity of A relative to B is v_AB = v_A - v_B. This formula gives the velocity of object A as observed from object B's reference frame.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two cars moving at 60 and 40 km/h in the same direction: relative velocity?

options.text: 100 km/h
50 km/h
20 km/h

quiz_questions.explanation: For objects moving in the same direction, relative velocity = 60 - 40 = 20 km/h. The faster car appears to move at 20 km/h as seen from the slower car.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an inertial frame?

options.text: A non-accelerating frame where Newton's laws apply directly
Any frame attached to Earth
A rotating frame
A frame moving faster than light

quiz_questions.explanation: An inertial frame is a reference frame that is not accelerating (moving at constant velocity or at rest). Newton's laws of motion hold directly in inertial frames without needing corrections.

quiz_questions.question_text: When does Galilean relativity break down?

options.text: Only in non-inertial reference frames
It never breaks down under any conditions
At speeds close to the speed of light
At very low speeds near absolute zero

quiz_questions.explanation: Galilean relativity (simple velocity addition) breaks down at speeds approaching the speed of light (3 x 10^8 m/s). At those speeds, Einstein's special relativity must be used instead.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why does rain appear angled when you run?

options.text: Your motion changes the rain's velocity relative to you
The wind always blows horizontally
Rain never falls straight
It's an optical illusion

quiz_questions.explanation: When you run, your horizontal velocity adds to the rain's vertical velocity in your reference frame, creating a diagonal (angled) relative velocity. The rain appears to come at you from an angle.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a river crossing, the boat's ground velocity is found by:

options.text: Using only the boat's speed
Dividing boat speed by current speed
Vector sum of boat velocity relative to water and water velocity relative to ground
Subtracting all velocities

quiz_questions.explanation: The boat's velocity relative to the ground equals the vector sum of its velocity relative to the water plus the water's velocity relative to the ground: v_BG = v_BW + v_WG.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two cars approach each other, one at 50 km/h and the other at 60 km/h. What is their relative speed?

options.text: 10 km/h
55 km/h
110 km/h

quiz_questions.explanation: When two objects move toward each other (opposite directions), their relative speed is the sum: 50 + 60 = 110 km/h. Each car sees the other approaching at 110 km/h.

quiz_questions.question_text: Is it possible for an object to be at rest in one frame but moving in another?

options.text: No, an object is either moving or at rest regardless of frame
Yes, motion depends on the reference frame of the observer
Only if the object is in outer space far from any planet
Only if the object is accelerating at the same time

quiz_questions.explanation: Motion is relative. A passenger sitting on a moving train is at rest in the train's frame but moving in the ground frame. There is no absolute state of motion or rest.

quiz_questions.question_text: A person walks at 2 m/s on a train moving at 30 m/s in the same direction. What is the person's speed relative to the ground?

options.text: 28 m/s
32 m/s
30 m/s
60 m/s

quiz_questions.explanation: When moving in the same direction, velocities add: 30 + 2 = 32 m/s relative to the ground. The person's ground speed is the sum of the train speed and walking speed.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'relative motion' mean?

options.text: The motion of one object as observed from the perspective of another object
The motion of an object that is related to a family member of the observer
The motion of an object that occurs only relative to the center of Earth
The motion of an object that is approximately but not exactly measured

quiz_questions.explanation: Relative motion describes how one object moves as seen from the perspective of another. All motion is relative because there is no absolute fixed point in the universe.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'relative velocity' mean?

options.text: The velocity of one object as measured in the reference frame of another object
The average velocity calculated by dividing total displacement by total time
The velocity that remains constant when no forces act on an object
The velocity an object must have to escape Earth's gravitational pull

quiz_questions.explanation: Relative velocity is the velocity of object A as observed from the reference frame of object B. It is calculated as v_AB = v_A - v_B.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'observer' mean in the context of reference frames?

options.text: The person or object whose reference frame is being used to measure motion
A special instrument used in laboratories to measure very fast objects
A person who watches an experiment but does not participate in it at all
The fastest-moving object in any given physics problem about motion

quiz_questions.explanation: In physics, an observer is the person or object whose reference frame is used to make measurements. Different observers in different frames may measure different velocities for the same object.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'non-inertial frame'?

options.text: An accelerating reference frame where Newton's laws need correction terms
A reference frame that is perfectly at rest and never moves at all
A reference frame used only in outer space far from any planets
A reference frame that moves at constant velocity in a straight line

quiz_questions.explanation: A non-inertial frame is accelerating (speeding up, slowing down, or rotating). Newton's laws do not directly apply in non-inertial frames without adding fictitious forces like the centrifugal force.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'Galilean velocity addition' refer to?

options.text: The rule that velocities simply add or subtract between reference frames at everyday speeds
The theory that heavier objects fall faster than lighter ones in Earth's gravity
The principle that all objects in free fall accelerate at exactly 9.8 m/s^2
The law that says no object can exceed the speed of light in a vacuum

quiz_questions.explanation: Galilean velocity addition says that velocities in different reference frames simply add or subtract: v_AG = v_AB + v_BG. This works well at everyday speeds but breaks down near the speed of light.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does it mean to say 'all motion is relative'?

options.text: Motion can only be described with respect to a chosen reference point or frame
All objects in the universe are constantly moving and can never be at rest
Motion is only an illusion and no objects actually change their position
All objects move at the same speed relative to the center of the universe

quiz_questions.explanation: Saying 'all motion is relative' means there is no absolute state of motion or rest. Whether an object is moving or stationary depends entirely on what reference frame you choose.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'ground frame' or 'Earth frame' mean?

options.text: A reference frame that treats the surface of the Earth as stationary
A reference frame located at the exact center of Earth's core
A frame used only for objects that are in contact with the ground
A frame that can only measure horizontal motion along the ground

quiz_questions.explanation: The ground frame (Earth frame) treats the surface of the Earth as stationary. Most everyday measurements of position and velocity are made in this frame.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'fictitious force' mean in a non-inertial frame?

options.text: An apparent force that arises from the acceleration of the reference frame itself
A force that is completely imaginary and has no measurable physical effect
A real force that only exists in outer space far from any gravitational bodies
A force that scientists invented to make their equations look more balanced

quiz_questions.explanation: Fictitious (pseudo) forces appear in non-inertial (accelerating) frames to account for the frame's acceleration. Examples include centrifugal force and the Coriolis force. They have real, measurable effects within that frame.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'velocity addition' mean?

options.text: Combining velocities from different reference frames to find velocity in a third frame
Adding together the speeds of two objects that collide and stick together
Increasing an object's velocity by applying a constant force over a period of time
Calculating the total distance by multiplying speed by time for each segment

quiz_questions.explanation: Velocity addition is the process of combining velocities measured in different frames. For example, v_AC = v_AB + v_BC connects velocity of A relative to B with velocity of B relative to C.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'speed of light' and why is it important for relative motion?

options.text: About 3 x 10^8 m/s; it is the speed limit where Galilean addition breaks down
About 340 m/s; it is the speed at which relative motion can no longer be measured
About 9.8 m/s; it determines how fast objects fall in free fall near Earth
About 1000 m/s; it is the maximum speed at which sound travels through air

quiz_questions.explanation: The speed of light is approximately 3 x 10^8 m/s. At speeds approaching this value, Galilean velocity addition no longer works and must be replaced by Einstein's relativistic formulas.

quiz_questions.question_text: A passenger on a train moving at 25 m/s throws a ball forward at 5 m/s relative to the train. What is the ball's speed relative to the ground?

options.text: 20 m/s
30 m/s
25 m/s
5 m/s

quiz_questions.explanation: The ball's ground velocity = train velocity + ball's velocity relative to train = 25 + 5 = 30 m/s forward. Both velocities are in the same direction, so they add.

quiz_questions.question_text: A boat heads north at 4 m/s in still water. The river current flows east at 3 m/s. What is the boat's actual speed relative to the ground?

options.text: 7 m/s
1 m/s
5 m/s
12 m/s

quiz_questions.explanation: North and east are perpendicular, so use the Pythagorean theorem: speed = sqrt(4^2 + 3^2) = sqrt(16 + 9) = sqrt(25) = 5 m/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student on a moving bus throws a ball straight up. From the student's perspective, the ball goes up and comes back down. What does a person standing on the sidewalk see?

options.text: The ball goes straight up and straight down, the same as the student sees
The ball follows a parabolic arc because it has both vertical and horizontal velocity
The ball flies backward behind the bus because the bus moves out from under it
The ball stays perfectly still in midair while the bus moves forward beneath it

quiz_questions.explanation: From the ground, the ball has both vertical velocity (from the throw) and horizontal velocity (from the bus), so it follows a parabolic path. Different frames see different trajectories.

quiz_questions.question_text: A plane flies north at 200 km/h relative to the air. A wind blows from west to east at 50 km/h. What is the plane's speed relative to the ground?

options.text: 150 km/h
250 km/h
About 206 km/h
About 194 km/h

quiz_questions.explanation: The northward and eastward velocities are perpendicular: speed = sqrt(200^2 + 50^2) = sqrt(40000 + 2500) = sqrt(42500) = approximately 206 km/h.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student walks backward at 1 m/s on a moving walkway that travels forward at 2 m/s. What is the student's velocity relative to the ground?

options.text: 3 m/s forward
1 m/s forward
1 m/s backward
2 m/s forward

quiz_questions.explanation: Walking backward at 1 m/s on a walkway moving forward at 2 m/s: ground velocity = 2 + (-1) = 1 m/s forward. The walkway moves faster forward than the student walks backward.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two trains run on parallel tracks. Train A moves east at 80 km/h and Train B moves east at 60 km/h. A passenger on Train B looks at Train A. How fast does Train A appear to move?

options.text: 140 km/h east
20 km/h east
80 km/h east
20 km/h west

quiz_questions.explanation: Relative velocity of A as seen from B = v_A - v_B = 80 - 60 = 20 km/h east. Train A appears to slowly move ahead of Train B at 20 km/h.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student sits in a car at a stoplight. The car next to them starts to roll backward. The student briefly feels like their own car is moving forward. What explains this?

options.text: The student's car actually did move forward slightly due to engine vibration
Relative motion makes the student perceive their car moving when the other car moves
Gravity pulled the student's car forward when the other car moved backward
The road surface tilted slightly causing both cars to shift position

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a classic example of relative motion perception. When the neighboring car rolls backward, the relative motion between the two cars is the same as if the student's car moved forward.

quiz_questions.question_text: A swimmer swims at 1.5 m/s in still water and needs to cross a river with a 2 m/s current. Can the swimmer reach a point directly across the river?

options.text: Yes, by swimming straight across and ignoring the current entirely
No, because the current speed exceeds the swimmer's speed in still water
Yes, by swimming at exactly the same speed as the river current
No, because you cannot swim across any river with a current at all

quiz_questions.explanation: To reach a point directly across, the swimmer would need to aim upstream enough to cancel the current. Since the current (2 m/s) exceeds the swimmer's speed (1.5 m/s), they cannot fully compensate and will be carried downstream.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student on a skateboard moving at 3 m/s throws a ball backward at 3 m/s relative to themselves. What is the ball's velocity relative to the ground?

options.text: 6 m/s forward
3 m/s backward
0 m/s (the ball drops straight down)
3 m/s forward

quiz_questions.explanation: Ground velocity = skateboard velocity + ball's velocity relative to skateboard = 3 + (-3) = 0 m/s. The ball momentarily has zero horizontal velocity and drops straight down from the ground observer's perspective.

quiz_questions.question_text: A helicopter hovers above a moving truck. A package is dropped from the helicopter. Where does the package land relative to the truck?

options.text: Directly on the truck because the package has no horizontal velocity
Behind the truck because the truck moves forward while the package falls
In front of the truck because air resistance pushes the package ahead
It lands exactly where it was dropped because gravity acts only downward

quiz_questions.explanation: The hovering helicopter has zero horizontal velocity, so the dropped package also has zero horizontal velocity. The truck moves forward while the package falls straight down, so the package lands behind the truck.
